purpose: Summarize the final tradeoff between TTL-only and tag-aware cache invalidation.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content: Summarize the final tradeoff between TTL-only and tag-aware cache invalidation.

sections.title: Project
sections.content: catalog-cache-branch-merge: Cognitive repo for evaluating cache invalidation strategies with branches

sections.title: Goals
sections.content: - [Active] Choose a cache invalidation strategy for the product catalog: Compare two candidate approaches and keep the final architecture decision auditable across branches.

sections.title: Tasks
sections.content:
- [Done] Compare branch outcomes and choose the final cache design: Use separate cognitive branches to evaluate TTL-only and tag-aware invalidation before deciding on main.
- [Done] Evaluate tag-aware invalidation: Test whether targeted invalidation provides the consistency needed for catalog updates.
- [Done] Evaluate TTL-only invalidation: Measure the simplicity and freshness risk of expiring catalog entries only by time window.

sections.title: Hypotheses
sections.content:
- [Supported] (88%) Evaluating each cache strategy in its own cognitive branch will make the final architecture decision easier to justify and review.
- [Supported] (86%) Tag-aware invalidation is the better catalog strategy because it preserves freshness without invalidating unrelated entries.
- [Refuted] (62%) TTL-only invalidation is sufficient for the catalog because operational simplicity outweighs short-lived staleness.

sections.title: Decisions
sections.content:
- [Rejected] Do not choose TTL-only as the final catalog strategy: The strategy is attractive for simplicity but does not meet the freshness requirement for price and stock updates.
- [Accepted] Treat tag-aware invalidation as the preferred candidate: The strategy keeps catalog reads coherent after updates and the executable test confirms the targeted invalidation behavior.
- [Accepted] Choose tag-aware invalidation for the catalog cache: The branch comparison is now explicit on main, and the tag-aware line offers the strongest consistency story with executable evidence.

sections.title: Evidence
sections.content:
- [Experiment] (99%) Executable tests confirm targeted invalidation works: The catalog cache tests verify that invalidating the boots category clears only related entries while preserving unrelated products.
- [Observation] (96%) Tag-aware branch carries stronger consistency evidence: The selected branch combines targeted invalidation semantics with passing tests, while the TTL-only branch records unresolved freshness risk.
- [Observation] (94%) Both candidate branches are preserved on main for comparison: Main now contains the TTL-only and tag-aware reasoning lines, which makes the final cache choice auditable instead of conversational.
- [Observation] (91%) TTL-only leaves stale catalog risk unresolved: The TTL-only strategy still allows stale price or stock reads until expiration, which is risky for a freshness-sensitive catalog.
- [Document] (90%) Tag-aware strategy supports targeted invalidation: The strategy note records that related products can be invalidated immediately by tag instead of waiting for expiration.
- [Document] (84%) TTL-only keeps the implementation minimal: The TTL-only strategy note shows the smallest operational surface: entries expire by time without explicit invalidation logic.

sections.title: Conclusions
sections.content:
- [Accepted] TTL-only remains useful as a low-cost baseline, but it is not strong enough for the catalog path that needs targeted freshness.
- [Accepted] Tag-aware invalidation provides the best balance for the catalog because it keeps freshness high without clearing unrelated entries.
